You'll Learn React Eventually, So Why Not Now? - React Components & Props - (6)
Time to talk about components. If you're a Vue developer, the component concept is probably second nature to you already. So what about React? How do React components actually work?
In Vue, we all know the saying: everything is a component (
). We can break any chunk of the page into a small, self-contained component and maintain it on its own. The classic examples are the ones people split out the most:

Thanks to this idea of componentization, we can pull out the parts that get reused a lot, maintain them and style them independently, and reuse them everywhere — which cuts down on the coupling between bits of code.
The official Vue.js site even has a picture illustrating this:
So really, you can think of a component as basically the idea of modularization.
How exactly you decide to split things into components is out of scope for this post, so let's jump straight into the React side of things.
No matter which of the VAR frameworks (Vue, Angular, React) you pick, they all have the concept of components. So let's get to know React's take on it.

Class isn't the style I'm covering this time, but both ways of writing it are a form of Component.

be uppercase. Otherwise JSX can easily mistake it for an HTML DOM element. For example, if you create a Component called

Props are the core trick when it comes to passing data between components. When we pull out a repeated chunk into its own component, we use Props to hand data over to that component. Here's a Vue props example — we usually pair it with
See the Pen (55)Vue Nested Components by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.

like Vue does to pass it along:
See the Pen (56) React - Nested Components by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
(Since JSX can't output an object directly to the screen, you have to run it through
In React you can pass more than just plain strings — objects, arrays, numbers, even DOM all work:
See the Pen (58) React - Components Props by Ray (@hsiangfeng) on CodePen.
There's one genuinely fun thing in the example above: if what you pass in is an HTML DOM, it gets rendered straight to the screen once it reaches the component. Kind of neat.

Last thing, and this one's really important: in React, Props are part of a one-way data flow. What does that mean? In short, Props are read-only. This is the same as Vue — it's mainly there to avoid the data flow getting messy, so as a rule you're not supposed to modify Props.
Remember this? When writing Vue components, we sometimes use the Slot feature to pass data along, like this:

So you might be wondering: if we already have Props, why bother with Slot? Picture this scenario. A site often uses a ton of dialog/popup windows, but the content, behavior, and styles might all be different. Sure, you could build each popup as a component and use Props to pass data in and render the screen — but then every single popup would need to be wrapped up as its own component, and we can't always know up front what content, style, or behavior will get passed in. That's exactly when the Slot trick comes in handy.
Here's a Bootstrap Modal as an example:

Modal title

Modal body text goes here.

block alone tends to vary a lot. One moment it's a Save + Reset button pair, the next it's Edit + Cancel, and so on. So if we could decide what that block is going to show
we even inject the component, instead of switching it with Props, Slot is actually really handy.
But React doesn't have a Slot tag like Vue does. What we can do, though, is use the

Time to talk about components. If you're a Vue developer, the component concept is probably second nature to you already. So what about React? How do React components work? In this post we'll walk through React components, nested components, Props (and the fact that they're read-only), and how to fake Vue's slot behavior using children.
